title: Lua Essentials
description: Learn Lua from tables and functions through metatables, coroutines, and string patterns.

sections.title: Variables & Types
sections.code_snippet:
local x = 10
local y = 'hello'
print(type(x), type(y))
sections.explanation: In Lua, all numbers are of type 'number' (doubles by default) and text is 'string'.

sections.title: Table as Array
sections.explanation: Lua arrays are 1-indexed! `t[1]` is 10. `#t` returns the length (3).

sections.title: Table as Dictionary
sections.explanation: Dot notation `person.name` accesses the 'name' key in the table, returning 'Alice'.

sections.title: Nil Check
sections.explanation: Accessing a non-existent key in a Lua table returns `nil` (not an error).

sections.title: Functions as Values
sections.code_snippet:
local add = function(a, b) return a + b end
print(add(3, 4))
sections.explanation: Functions are first-class values in Lua. The anonymous function is assigned to `add` and called normally.

sections.title: Metatables __index
sections.explanation: `__index` metamethod is called when a key isn't found. It falls back to `defaults`, finding 'red'.

sections.title: Metatables __add
sections.code_snippet:
local mt = {__add = function(a, b)
    return {value = a.value + b.value}
end}
local x = setmetatable({value = 10}, mt)
local y = setmetatable({value = 20}, mt)
local z = x + y
print(z.value)
sections.explanation: The `__add` metamethod defines the `+` operator for tables. 10 + 20 = 30.

sections.title: Coroutine Basics
sections.code_snippet:
local co = coroutine.create(function()
    coroutine.yield(1)
    coroutine.yield(2)
    return 3
end)
local _, a = coroutine.resume(co)
local _, b = coroutine.resume(co)
print(a, b)
sections.explanation: Each `resume` runs until the next `yield`. First resume yields 1, second yields 2.

sections.title: String Patterns
sections.explanation: `%d+` matches one or more digits. The first (and only) digit sequence in the string is '123'.

sections.title: Module Pattern
sections.body_markdown: What is the idiomatic way to create a module in Lua?

choices.B: Create a local table, add functions to it, then return it

sections.explanation: The modern Lua module pattern is: `local M = {}`, add functions as `M.func = ...`, then `return M`.
